summary: Gruber examines Sam Altman's sudden firing from OpenAI, piecing together reports from Kevin Roose and Kara Swisher to paint a picture of a deep philosophical split within the company. The board, led by chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, ousted Altman for pursuing aggressive commercialization at odds with OpenAI's nonprofit charter. Both Altman and board chairman Greg Brockman were blindsided, with Brockman learning of the firing just five minutes before it happened. Gruber finds the board's shock at Altman's direction laughable, given how openly he had been steering the company toward profit and speed. He compares it to the famous Casablanca scene of feigned surprise.
key_points: The firing reflects a fundamental philosophical split between Sutskever's cautious, charter-faithful faction and Altman/Brockman's move-fast-and-monetize approach
Board chairman Brockman learned of the CEO's firing just 5 minutes before it happened — an extraordinary breach of corporate governance norms
OpenAI's charter is a mere 423 words advocating cautious, humanity-first AI development that bears no resemblance to the company Altman was building
Gruber finds it absurd that the board only now realized Altman was leading the company contrary to its own stated mission
money_quotes: I've never once heard of a corporate board firing the company's CEO behind the back of the chairman of the board.
Sutskever and the OpenAI board seemingly see Altman/Brockman as reckless swashbucklers; Altman and Brockman, I suspect, see Sutskever and his side as a bunch of ninnies.
It's a mere 423 words, and very plainly written. It doesn't sound anything at all like the company Altman has been running.
How in the world it took them until now to realize Altman was leading OpenAI in directions completely contrary to their charter is beyond me.
It's like the police chief in Casablanca being 'shocked — shocked!' to find out that gambling was taking place in a casino where he played.
key_insight: OpenAI's board fired Altman for violating the company's charter, but the real absurdity is that his commercially aggressive direction was obvious to everyone for years.
